advantages: Averages model and hyperparameter comparisons across multiple validation folds—reduces overfitting to one lucky partition when n is small.
Surfaces fold-to-fold instability (high std) so overfit-prone high-variance models are visible before deployment.
Nested CV separates tuning from scoring, mitigating optimism when many settings compete on the same inner folds.
Reuses nearly all development rows for both training and validation across rounds—efficient selection under limited labeled data.
disadvantages: Inner CV plus outer nested loops multiply compute—costly for large grids or slow base learners.
CV for selection still does not regularize the model; it only improves the choice process—pair with L1/L2, early stopping, or simpler models when needed.
Repeated tuning on the same development set without nested structure can still yield optimistic development scores.
Plain shuffled k-fold can be optimistic for grouped, temporal, or user-clustered data—wrong splitter undermines mitigation.
